steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence joins two independent clauses with the transition word "though." The question asks which punctuation correctly sets off "though" between those two clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — comma before "though," semicolon after: a comma separates "though" from the first clause; a semicolon after "though" marks the start of the second independent clause. This comma–though–semicolon pattern correctly bridges two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice B — no punctuation before "though": without a comma, the first clause runs directly into the transition with no boundary, creating a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — semicolons on both sides: a semicolon before "though" treats it as a conjunctive adverb, but "though" is not typically used that way; this creates awkward punctuation. ✗
  • Choice D — comma on both sides: placing commas on both sides makes "though" a parenthetical rather than a transitional connector, which misrepresents its function here. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
